explanation.text: The rapid list of movements—kicking, soaring, whirling, and flying—creates a vivid picture of Kathleen bursting with energy across the stage. This spirited imagery supports choice C rather than focusing on how quickly the dance ends or whether she receives recognition.

Kathleen hardly knows where she is and barely notices the audience, the hall, or even the judge while she dances. Because those surroundings have faded from her attention, oblivious to means unaware of.

Kathleen becomes absorbed in the joy of the dance and wishes the music would never stop. This delight makes the audience, unfamiliar hall, judge, and Tess O’Hara disappear from her thoughts for a while, which supports choice B.

Even after hearing the audience praise her, Kathleen loses interest and searches for Polly. She wants Polly to describe the performance to her mother, which shows that her success matters most when she can share it with her family.

Kathleen says that neither she nor the other girls can adequately describe the performance to her mother. She believes Polly will be able to tell it with panache, which directly explains why Polly’s presence is so important to her.

Kathleen keeps smiling at Mrs. Maguire, so she continues to treat her teacher politely. At the same time, she looks around the teacher for Polly, which shows that her attention is elsewhere even as she maintains the conversation.

In lines 1–32, Kathleen’s feelings and reactions are conveyed through her first-person narration and descriptions of the dance. Lines 33–61 add spoken comments and conversations with Mrs. Maguire and Polly, so her thoughts are then revealed through both description and dialogue.

Most stanzas show creatures responding to the fading day: birds seek nests, flowers close, cattle return home, and deer settle to sleep. These repeated details support the idea that the poem mainly describes animals preparing for nightfall.
The opening exclamation calls evening pleasant even as the clouds hide the sun. That approving, energetic greeting reveals enthusiasm for this time of day rather than alarm or disapproval.
The stock-doves sit within the leafy wood, where the leaves hide and protect them from view. This context shows that screened means sheltered or concealed, matching definition 2.

The nightingale tells the selfsame tale that it sang when the ancient earth was young. By connecting its present song to the distant past, these lines provide evidence that the event in nature has repeated for many years.
Lines 11–13 say that the flowers close one by one, with the lily and rose gradually shutting their petals. The phrase one by one demonstrates a change unfolding step by step during the evening.

Throughout the evening, creatures nest, leap, eat, return home, emerge, hunt, and sing. Although the scene feels calm, this variety of activity shows that busy best describes what most of the animals are doing.

Lines 56–60 explicitly announce that evening is done and night has come. The statement that the great calm has ceased shows that night is a new stage, clearly distinguishing it from the evening described before.

Lines 6–14 explain that reading builds attention, sequential thinking, and the ability to connect causes with effects. The discussion of childhood brain flexibility and improvement through practice shows how reading can affect the mind over time.

Greenfield says that children’s brains have more plasticity, or ability to grow and change. Because young children can form new thinking skills more readily, parents should encourage reading while new ideas and abilities are easier to grasp.

The article says that readers’ brains activate as though they were actually experiencing the described sights, sounds, smells, and tastes. In this context, simulate means to imitate or re-create a real experience.

The study found low levels of disease-related plaques in older adults who had read, played chess, or written letters since childhood. This evidence illustrates how brain-stimulating activities may have a protective effect against brain disease.

The MRI study found that imagining a book’s landscapes, sounds, smells, and tastes activates the same brain regions used for those real experiences. This evidence supports Stein’s claim by showing that reading triggers many different brain functions.

The article develops evidence that reading improves attention and empathy, reduces stress, and may protect the brain. The statement in choice B introduces this broad idea by saying that reading offers benefits beyond pleasure, literacy, and factual knowledge.

The author presents studies and expert testimony about reading’s mental, social, and physical benefits. This collection of evidence is meant to convince readers that reading is worthwhile, not merely to advertise her books or compare forms of entertainment.

Lines 10–13 describe scientists changing crop DNA so the crops survive glyphosate. Lines 18–21 extend that idea by explaining that natural DNA changes can also let weeds survive the chemical, which shows that genetic changes can benefit both crops and weeds.
The author says that the chance of a helpful DNA change in any one weed is very, very small. That context indicates that impossibly improbable means highly unlikely.

The lottery comparison explains that any individual weed has only a tiny chance of developing resistance, but billions of repeated chances eventually produce a winner. This evidence supports the conclusion that useful DNA changes can occur by chance.

The section explains how repeated exposure made rare resistant weeds increasingly likely and then names rigid ryegrass, horseweed, and at least twenty-one resistant species. Those examples support the main idea that several kinds of weeds developed resistance to glyphosate.

Winning the lottery introduces the general idea that rare DNA changes eventually produce glyphosate-resistant weeds. Copy that then focuses on palmer pigweed and explains how it develops and spreads resistance, providing a specific example of that main idea.

Resistant pigweed carries extra copies of the gene that makes glyphosate’s target enzyme. Because the plant produces more enzyme than the chemical can block, these lines demonstrate how weeds can evolve around a threat and why fighting them is difficult.

The seed destructor is described alongside herbicide mixtures, flattened rye, and plowing seeds deeper underground. Adding this different mechanical approach supports the author’s point that farmers need a variety of methods because no single solution protects every crop.
